question: How do ReAct-style agents differ from planner-executor agents?

heuristic_evaluation.question: How do ReAct-style agents differ from planner-executor agents?

claim_lines: ReAct-style agents keep reasoning and acting in a single interleaved loop: the model emits thoughts that can create, track, and update action plans, then acts to fetch new information from the environment. Planner-executor agents split that loop into a planning phase and an execution phase. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))

Tool-use implication: ReAct treats tool calls as part of the same thought-action-observation trace, so the model can revise after each observation; planner-executor makes tool calls downstream of a prior plan, which is easier to route, chain, checkpoint, and subject to approval. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))

Failure modes differ: ReAct’s paper explicitly discusses prompting limits and notes hallucination/error propagation in reasoning-heavy settings, while PEAR reports that planner quality matters more than executor quality and that prompt-injection on planner-executor communication or planner prompts can cause substantial end-to-end failures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))

Prefer ReAct when the task is open-ended, information is uncertain, and you want a tight think-act-observe loop; prefer planner-executor when the task benefits from upfront decomposition, sequential handoff, observability, or human approval before tool use. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))
- interleaved reasoning and acting — ReAct generates reasoning traces and actions in an interleaved manner. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))

- explicit planning stage — planner-executor separates a planner that decomposes the request into subtasks from an executor that performs them. ([aclanthology.org](https://aclanthology.org/2026.findings-eacl.237.pdf))

- tool-use implications — ReAct uses tools inside the same loop; planner-executor centralizes tool calls in the executor and supports chaining/approval. ([learn.microsoft.com](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/agent-framework/workflows/advanced/agent-executor?utm_source=openai))
- failure modes — ReAct can suffer hallucination/error propagation; planner-executor is vulnerable to weak planners and planner-channel injection. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))
- when each architecture is preferable — ReAct for uncertain, reactive tasks; planner-executor for multi-stage workflows needing control and review. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2210.03629))

coverage_audit.reason: Clearly explains ReAct as a single interleaved think-act-observe loop.
Clearly contrasts planner-executor as having a separate planning phase before execution.
Explains that ReAct tool calls are embedded in the loop while planner-executor centralizes calls in execution; mentions routing, chaining, checkpointing, and approval.
Mentions hallucination/error propagation for ReAct and weak-planner plus prompt-injection risks for planner-executor, though the evidence support is uneven.
Gives reasonable guidance on when to prefer each architecture, including uncertainty/open-endedness vs. decomposition/control/approval.

citation_audit.claim: ReAct-style agents keep reasoning and acting in a single interleaved loop ... Planner-executor agents split that loop into a planning phase and an execution phase.
citation_audit.reason: The ReAct paper supports interleaved reasoning/acting, and the planner-executor paper supports the explicit planning/execution split.

citation_audit.claim: Tool-use implication: ReAct treats tool calls as part of the same thought-action-observation trace ... planner-executor makes tool calls downstream of a prior plan, which is easier to route, chain, checkpoint, and subject to approval.
citation_audit.reason: The first part is plausible from ReAct; the routing/chaining/checkpointing/approval benefits are more about framework docs and workflow design than a direct ReAct-vs-planner-executor comparison.

citation_audit.claim: Failure modes differ: ReAct’s paper explicitly discusses prompting limits and notes hallucination/error propagation in reasoning-heavy settings, while PEAR reports that planner quality matters more than executor quality and that prompt-injection on planner-executor communication or planner prompts can cause substantial end-to-end failures.
citation_audit.reason: The ReAct limitation is directionally supported, but the PEAR-specific robustness claim is not well anchored in the inline citation pattern shown; it would need tighter source attribution.

citation_audit.claim: Prefer ReAct when the task is open-ended, information is uncertain, and you want a tight think-act-observe loop; prefer planner-executor when the task benefits from upfront decomposition, sequential handoff, observability, or human approval before tool use.
citation_audit.reason: This is a reasonable engineering recommendation, but it is partly inferential rather than directly evidenced by the cited sources.

source_audit.reason: Primary ReAct paper; highly relevant for interleaved reasoning/acting and some limitations.
Peer-reviewed technical comparison/benchmark source relevant to planner-executor behavior and failure modes.
Useful framework documentation for executor-style orchestration and tool-use workflow implications, though vendor-specific.
Relevant documentation for sequential orchestration patterns and handoff/approval behavior.
Relevant overview of orchestration patterns, but still framework-specific.
Secondary summary site; less authoritative than primary papers or framework docs.

judge_evaluation.summary: The answer covers all required aspects and gives a generally correct comparison, but some of the more specific tool-use and failure-mode claims are only weakly grounded or partly inferential.
judge_evaluation.recommended_fix: Tighten the evidence chain: cite the exact source for each failure-mode and workflow claim, and separate what is directly stated in sources from what is a reasonable engineering inference.
